sections.title: Fixed Income Securities
sections.content: Fixed income securities represent debt obligations that pay periodic interest and return principal at maturity. Key types include government bonds, corporate bonds, and municipal bonds.
keyPoints: Coupon rate: stated interest rate on bond
Par value: face value repaid at maturity
Zero-coupon bonds pay no periodic interest

practiceQuestions.question: A zero-coupon bond:
practiceQuestions.explanation: Zero-coupon bonds do not pay periodic interest. They are issued at a discount and pay par value at maturity.

practiceQuestions.question: The coupon rate of a bond is:
options: The stated interest rate on the bond
practiceQuestions.explanation: The coupon rate is the stated annual interest rate on the bond, expressed as a percentage of par value.

sections.title: Bond Valuation
sections.content: Bond prices are determined by discounting future cash flows (coupons and principal) at the market required rate of return. Bond prices and yields move inversely.
keyPoints: Bond price = PV of coupons + PV of principal
When market rate > coupon rate, bond trades at discount
When market rate < coupon rate, bond trades at premium
Price and yield move in opposite directions

practiceQuestions.question: If market interest rates rise, existing bond prices will:
practiceQuestions.explanation: Bond prices and interest rates move inversely. When market rates rise, existing bonds with lower coupon rates become less attractive, causing their prices to fall.

practiceQuestions.question: A bond with a 5% coupon rate when market rates are 6% will trade at:
practiceQuestions.explanation: When the coupon rate (5%) is below the market rate (6%), the bond must trade at a discount to compensate investors for the lower coupon payments.

sections.title: Duration and Convexity
sections.content: Duration measures a bond's sensitivity to interest rate changes. Convexity provides a more accurate measure for larger rate changes.
keyPoints: Duration: approximate % price change for 1% rate change

practiceQuestions.question: A bond with duration of 5 years will approximately change in price by what percentage if rates increase by 1%?
practiceQuestions.explanation: %ΔPrice ≈ -5 × 1% = -5%. The negative sign indicates price falls when rates rise.
